coredata.dc:description: Traumatic spinal cord injury (SCI) patients face severe oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, and intense inflammation, leading to neuronal degeneration and hindering recovery. Traditional therapies often target only one aspect of this complex pathology, yielding suboptimal results. To overcome this, we developed an eco-friendly therapeutic strategy using memory-driven Ce-enhanced carbon Dots derived from vegetable moss, called V-CDs@Ce. Uniquely, our approach introduces Ce to leverage its antioxidant enzyme-like activity, but does not retain Ce in the final material, overcoming the common issue of reduced biocompatibility caused by cerium oxides. This memory of Ce’s catalytic activity enables us to enhance the material’s antioxidant properties without compromising biological compatibility. V-CDs@Ce effectively mitigate mitochondrial damage, reduce neuronal apoptosis, and suppress inflammation and oxidative stress by promoting macrophage polarization from the pro-inflammatory M1 to the regenerative M2 phenotype, enhancing spinal cord repair and motor function recovery. Additionally, V-CDs@Ce restore the homeostasis of biometal ions, critical for traumatic SCI management, by modulating the AMPK/PGC-1α/Nrf-2 signaling pathway. The multifaceted therapeutic effects and ion-rebalancing properties of V-CDs@Ce represent a significant advancement in traumatic SCI treatment, offering a novel, comprehensive approach with improved biocompatibility.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of V-CDs and V-CDs@Ce. (a-b) Low and high magnification TEM images of V-CDs. (c-d) Low and high magnification TEM images of V-CDs@Ce. (e) UV–Vis absorption spectra of V-CDs and V-CDs@Ce. (f) FT-IR spectra of V-CDs and V-CDs@Ce. (g)^1H NMR spectra of V-CDs and V-CDs@Ce. (h) Fluorescence lifetime measurements with fitted curves for V-CDs (labeled V-CDs-F) and V-CDs@Ce (labeled V-CDs@Ce-F). (i) Emission spectra of V-CDs and V-CDs@Ce. (j) XPS survey spectra of V-CDs and V-CDs@Ce. (k-l) High-resolution C1s XPS spectra of V-CDs (k) and V-CDs@Ce (l). (m-o) ROS scavenging activities of V-CDs and V-CDs@Ce for 1O2, •OH and O2•- as studied by Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy. 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was used as the spin trap agent, with H2O2 and KO2 used as sources of •OH and 1O2, respectively. Superoxide anions were generated using KO2 dissolved in a dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and stearyl crown ether mixture. “Control” refers to treatments without V-CDs or V-CDs@Ce.

Inhibition of Neuronal Apoptosis by V-CDs and V-CDs@Ce. (a-b) Flow cytometry analysis of apoptosis rates in the H2O2 group, V-CDs group and V-CDs@Ce group, along with quantification of the apoptosis rates. (c, e) IF analysis of Cleaved caspase-3 in PC12 cells for each group, and quantification of the fluorescence intensity. (d, f) IF analysis of Cleaved caspase-3 in spinal cord tissues for each group, along with quantification of the fluorescence intensity. All data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3). * P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01, *** P < 0.001.

Attenuation of Neuronal Mitochondrial Damage and Oxidative Stress. (a-b) Quantification of ROS levels in PC12 cells in the H2O2 group, V-CDs group, and V-CDs@Ce group, as detected by the DCFH-DA probe. (c-d) Measurement and quantification of MMP in PC12 cells for each group using the JC-1 probe. (e) TEM observation of mitochondrial microstructure. (f) Relative ATP levels. (g) Evaluation of T-AOC, GSH, GSH-Px/GPX, SOD, POD, CAT, and MDA levels. All data are expressed as mean ± SD (n = 3). * P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01, *** P < 0.001.

Promotion of Microglial/Macrophage Polarization to the M2 Phenotype and its Anti-Inflammatory Consequences. (a-b) Quantification of ROS levels in BV2 cells within the LPS group, V-CDs group, and V-CDs@Ce group using the DCFH-DA probe, along with the quantification of ROS fluorescence intensity. (c-d) IF analysis of INOS expression levels in BV2 cells for each group, along with the quantification of their fluorescence intensity. (e-f) IF evaluation of Arg-1 expression levels in BV2 cells in each group, and quantification of its fluorescence intensity. (g-j) ELISA assessment of IL-6, IL-1β, TNF-α, and IL-10 levels in the LPS group, V-CDs group, and V-CDs@Ce group. (k) IHC determination of INOS levels in the spinal cord for each group. All data are expressed as mean ± SD (n = 3). * P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01, *** P < 0.001.

Enhanced Motor Function Recovery and Pathological Alleviation in Traumatic SCI Mouse Models. (a) Schematic diagram of the traumatic SCI model. (b) Footprints of mice from each group after 28 days (black for forelimbs and red for hindlimbs). (c) BMS scores of mice in each group on days 0, 3, 7, 14, 21, and 28. (d) Nissl staining of spinal cord tissues in each group. (e) HE staining of spinal cord tissues in each group. (f) HE staining of calf muscle gastrocnemius. (g) The CSA of the calf gastrocnemius muscle. (h) Relative mass of calf gastrocnemius muscle. All data are expressed as mean ± SD (n = 3). * P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01, *** P < 0.001. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Homeostatic Regulation of Diverse Metal Ions. The contents of calcium (a), copper (b), iron (c), sodium (d), manganese (e), magnesium (f), zinc (g), selenium (h), lithium (i) and potassium (j) in spinal cord (n = 6). (k-l) The expression and relative intensity of Nrf-2 in PC12 cells of each group were detected by IF (n = 3). (m-n) Western blotting analysis of P-AMPK, AMPK, PGC-1α, Nrf-2 levels in spinal cord tissues and their quantification (n = 3). * P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01, *** P < 0.001.

Enhancement of Wound Healing in Mouse Models. (a) Diagram illustrating mouse skin wound modeling and treatment. (b-c) Progression images of skin wound healing in each group on days 0, 1, 3, 5, 7, and 9, with quantification of wound area changes. (d) HE and Masson staining of skin tissues in each group. (e) IHC depicting CD31 expression in skin tissues of each group. (f-i) Levels of IL-6, IL-1β, TNF-α, and IL-10 in skin tissues. All data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3). * P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01, *** P < 0.001.

Long-Term Evaluation of Biocompatibility. (a) Schematic diagram of blood routine, blood biochemical tests and pathological assessment of major organs. (b) HE staining of heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys in mice treated with V-CDs@Ce after 28 days.
Schematic Diagram of V-CDs@Ce Synthesis Pathways and Therapeutic Mechanisms in Traumatic Spinal Cord Injury.

head.ce:title: Enzyme memory-driven Ce-enhanced carbon dots from vegetable moss enable rapid treatment of traumatic spinal cord injury

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Traumatic spinal cord injury (SCI) patients face severe oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, and intense inflammation, leading to neuronal degeneration and hindering recovery. Traditional therapies often target only one aspect of this complex pathology, yielding suboptimal results. To overcome this, we developed an eco-friendly therapeutic strategy using memory-driven Ce-enhanced carbon Dots derived from vegetable moss, called V-CDs@Ce. Uniquely, our approach introduces Ce to leverage its antioxidant enzyme-like activity, but does not retain Ce in the final material, overcoming the common issue of reduced biocompatibility caused by cerium oxides. This memory of Ce’s catalytic activity enables us to enhance the material’s antioxidant properties without compromising biological compatibility. V-CDs@Ce effectively mitigate mitochondrial damage, reduce neuronal apoptosis, and suppress inflammation and oxidative stress by promoting macrophage polarization from the pro-inflammatory M1 to the regenerative M2 phenotype, enhancing spinal cord repair and motor function recovery. Additionally, V-CDs@Ce restore the homeostasis of biometal ions, critical for traumatic SCI management, by modulating the AMPK/PGC-1α/Nrf-2 signaling pathway. The multifaceted therapeutic effects and ion-rebalancing properties of V-CDs@Ce represent a significant advancement in traumatic SCI treatment, offering a novel, comprehensive approach with improved biocompatibility.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Traumatic spinal cord injury (SCI) presents a significant medical challenge, marked by both primary and secondary injuries that initiate a complex cascade of pathological reactions [1]. These injuries often coexist with multiple wounds, further complicating the patient's condition [2]. Patients with traumatic SCI frequently experience prolonged immobility, which subjects their skin and soft tissues to continuous pressure, leading to the development of chronic wounds [3].

Traditional management of traumatic SCI and its associated wound complications typically involves the administration of multiple medications, each targeting different aspects of traumatic SCI and wound healing [4,5]. However, this approach is fraught with challenges, including potential drug contraindications and complications from polypharmacy [6]. The complexity of such treatment regimens delays timely intervention and places an additional burden on patients already coping with severe injuries [7]. As a result, these factors significantly hinder the overall recovery process for individuals dealing with both traumatic SCI and wound-related complications. Significant progress has been achieved in the application of nanomaterials for wound healing, including the development of hydrogels, nanoparticles, and nanoplatforms for targeted drug delivery [8–11]. Although various nanomedicines have shown promise in addressing either traumatic SCI recovery or wound healing, there remains a substantial gap in the simultaneous treatment of these dual complications [12–17].

The primary pathological changes in traumatic SCI include neuronal apoptosis and local inflammation [18]. Following traumatic SCI, patients are subjected to oxidative stress, inflammatory cascades, reduced enzymatic activity, and increased protein aggregation within the central nervous system (CNS), all contributing to neuronal mitochondrial damage [19]. The CNS harbors crucial metal ions such as iron, zinc, copper, selenium, and manganese, which are essential for various physiological processes [20–24]. The regulation of metal ion homeostasis is crucial for the optimal functioning of the body's antioxidant defense mechanisms, and any perturbation—whether resulting from excess or deficiency—can be deleterious to tissues. In pathological conditions, dysregulated metal ion metabolism can exacerbate toxicity and accelerate disease progression [25]. For example, an imbalance in copper ions has been associated with a novel form of programmed cell death known as cuproptosis [26]. Similarly, disturbances in other metal ions play significant roles in the onset and progression of traumatic SCI. Specifically, an imbalance in iron ions can induce ferroptosis in cells post-SCI, whereas supplementation with exogenous zinc or magnesium ions can facilitate recovery [27–30]. In the context of SCI, modulating iron homeostasis can inhibit lipid peroxidation of cell membranes, reduce intracellular oxidative stress, and prevent neuronal injury [18].

Mitochondria are pivotal in maintaining cellular ion homeostasis, particularly concerning calcium ions [31]. Furthermore, restoring mitochondrial calcium homeostasis can mitigate ion concentration gradients and prevent apoptosis caused by mitochondrial edema and dysfunction [32]. Mitochondria regulate calcium ion homeostasis through calcium ion channels and exchangers on their membranes, as well as through interactions with other organelles, such as the endoplasmic reticulum [33]. Disruption of metal ion homeostasis at the cellular level is closely linked to mitochondrial dysfunction [34–36]. Characterized by their double-membrane structure, mitochondria harbor numerous metal ion transport channels [37]. Damage to these organelles can disrupt biometal ion balance, leading to heavy metal accumulation and subsequent neuronal apoptosis [38].

Despite the advancements, traditional nanotherapies often fail to restore the homeostasis of multiple metal ions, which is a critical aspect of traumatic SCI pathology. Addressing this limitation is essential for developing more effective therapeutic strategies that can comprehensively manage metal ion imbalances and support mitochondrial function in SCI treatment.

To address these challenges, we have developed a novel therapeutic approach using Ce plays a pivotal role in enhancing enzymatic activity due to its ability to cycle between + 3 and + 4 oxidation states, facilitating redox reactions critical for antioxidant defense [39]. Despite these advantages, the presence of Ce typically poses challenges such as reduced biocompatibility, altered material size, and the potential for introducing side effects. To address this, we developed an innovative memory-driven Ce-enhanced approach.

In our strategy, Ce is introduced during the synthesis process to boost enzymatic activity, but it is subsequently removed from the final material. These marteirals were directly obtained by a one-pot hydrothermal method and Ce were removed by washing. Remarkably, even after the removal of Ce, the enhanced catalytic activity is retained, likely due to the polyphenolic matrix of the carbon dots, which may act as an imprinting system. This imprinting could preserve the structural memory of Ce’s enzymatic function, maintaining the material's antioxidant capacity without the adverse effects typically associated with residual Ce. The ability to “remember” Ce’s catalytic activity without retaining the actual Ce atoms represents a breakthrough in balancing material efficacy and safety.

V-CDs@Ce have demonstrated enhanced ability to mitigate inflammatory responses, reduce neuronal mitochondrial damage, prevent apoptosis, and enhance motor function recovery, thereby ameliorating pathological changes in experimental mouse models.

Crucially, our studies revealed that V-CDs@Ce not only promote synchronous wound healing in traumatic SCI-injured mice but also have profound effects on the homeostasis of multiple biometal ions, including calcium, copper, iron, sodium, manganese, magnesium, zinc, selenium, lithium and potassium. Further investigation uncovered that V-CDs@Ce exert their therapeutic effects by repairing damaged mitochondria through the AMPK/PGC-1α/Nrf-2 signaling pathway, thereby restoring metal ion homeostasis in vivo. Scheme 1 . Extensive experimental evaluations confirmed the long-term biocompatibility of V-CDs@Ce, providing a strong foundation for their potential clinical application.

By introducing this innovative memory-driven therapeutic approach that simultaneously addresses both traumatic SCI and wound complications, we aim to significantly enhance the quality of life and recovery outcomes for patients suffering from traumatic SCI. These findings underscore the potential of V-CDs@Ce as a powerful therapeutic agent and open new avenues for their application in clinical settings, offering promising prospects for improved outcomes in traumatic SCI patients.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of V-CDs and V-CDs@Ce
ce:section.ce:para: The process began with boiling 500 g of fresh vegetable moss in 2 L of water. After boiling, the solution was reduced by evaporation to a volume of 500 ml. From this concentrated solution, 80 ml were carefully transferred into a 100-milliliter Teflon tube, which was then subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 8 h in an oven. After this treatment, a brownish solution was obtained and centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 20 min to remove larger particles. The supernatant was further purified by passing it through a 0.22-µm syringe filter. The resulting solution, termed the final V-CDs solution, underwent dialysis using a tube with a molecular weight cutoff of 3000 KDa to further purify the material. The final product, now a yellow solution, was ready for various characterization experiments.

For the synthesis of V-CDs@Ce, the procedure was identical, except that 100 μL of 10 mM CeCl3 was added to the moss extract prior to the hydrothermal synthesis. During the dialysis step, Ce was carefully washed out, and its removal was confirmed through characterization techniques such as H Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (H NMR), X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer (ICP-MS), ensuring that no residual Ce remained to interfere with the results. This approach allowed the materials to retain the enzymatic memory of Ce without its physical presence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mouse husbandry, disease modeling and cellular modeling
ce:section.ce:para: Ninety female C57BL/6 J mice, aged 8 weeks and weighing approximately 20 g, were procured from Changsheng Biological Company in Liaoning, China. These mice were accommodated in a specific pathogen-free (SPF) animal laboratory at Jinzhou Medical University, where they were provided with adequate food, water, and suitable environmental conditions. Prior to the experiments, the mice were given one week to acclimatize to their new surroundings. The mice were anesthetized with pentobarbital sodium before the traumatic SCI model was established, then hair was removed from the surgical area and povidone iodine was used to disinfect the surgical area, after which a T9-T10 laminectomy was performed to expose the spinal cord and the Precision Systems and Instruments (PSI) HI-0400 spinal cord impinger (60 kdyn) was used to impinge the exposed spinal cord surface [40]. After the preparation of traumatic SCI model, spinal cord congestion, spasm of both lower limbs, tail spasm, and Basso Mouse Scale (BMS) score of 0 are considered as successful model. The sham group only had their laminas stripped, but no spinal cord blows. Subsequently, the mice received injections of 100 μL of V-CDs and V-CDs@Ce through the tail vein at a dosage of 4 mg/kg once a day. Antibiotics were administered to prevent infections, and the mice were manually voided until spontaneous voiding was restored. In addition, after shaving the dorsal hair, a 1.5 × 1.5 cm square mark was carefully delineated on the lower median back of both normal mice and mice with traumatic SCI. The skin was then fully excised along the marked area using a sterile, sharp blade. The created wound was subcutaneous and did not compromise the paraspinal fat or fascia. For treatment, the wounds were administered with 200 μL of V-CDs@Ce at a concentration of 400 μg/mL, applied via spraying three times daily. Conversely, the control group received an equivalent volume of sterile phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). To monitor the healing process, the wound sizes were documented through photography on days 0, 1, 3, 5, 7, and 9 post-treatment.These animal experiments were conducted with approval from the Ethics Committee of Jinzhou Medical University.

For cell culture, PC12 cells and BV2 cells were grown in high-glucose DMEM medium with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 4 % penicillin–streptomycin (PS) at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 environment. PC12 cells were exposed to 100 μmol/L of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) for 6 h to simulate the localized high oxidative environment in the spinal cord after SCI. BV2 cells were stimulated with 1 μg/mL of lipopolysaccharide (LPS) for 24 h to mimic the inflammatory environment. Subsequently, both cell types were treated with 100 μg/mL of V-CDs and V-CDs@Ce for 24 h.

After the mice were anesthetized, their hearts were injected into the PBS and paraformaldehyde (PFA). Subsequently, the spinal cord, heart, liver, spleen, lungs, kidneys, and brain of the mice were carefully extracted and placed in a solution of 4 % PFA for a period of 48 h. The tissues were then processed in two ways: Firstly, a portion of the tissue underwent dehydration through a gradient sedimentation process using sugar and was then subjected to frozen sectioning, resulting in sections with a thickness of 10 μm. Secondly, another portion of the tissue was dehydrated using an alcohol gradient, made transparent with xylene, and embedded in paraffin. This procedure led to paraffin sections with a thickness of 5 μm. The same procedure for removal was followed for the skin wounds of the mice.

IF staining was performed in accordance with established procedures [41]. Frozen sections from the spinal cord tissue of SCI mice after 3 days of treatment were used. The reagents and antibodies used included PBS, 4 % PFA, 0.1 % Triton-X, goat serum for sealing, 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI), anti-cleaved caspase-3 (affinity), anti-Inducible nitric oxide synthase (INOS) (affinity), anti-arginase (Arg-1) (affinity), anti-Neun (Abcam), anti-Nuclearrespiratoty factor 2 (Nrf-2) (affinity), Alexa Fluor 568 goat anti-mouse/rabbit IgG, and Alexa Fluor 488 goat anti-mouse/rabbit IgG (Invitrogen).

IHC staining was conducted following standard protocols. The reagents used included PBS, ethanol, xylene, 3 % H2O2, citrate buffer, serum for blocking, biotin-labeled goat anti-rabbit secondary antibody, HRP-labeled horseradish peroxidase, DAB chromogenic solution, hematoxylin staining solution, and neutral gum. The antibodies used were anti-INOS (affinity), anti-Arg-1 (affinity), and anti-CD31 (affinity).

Paraffin sections were prepared from the tissues of traumatic SCI mice after 28 days of treatment and mice with skin wounds after 9 days of treatment. These sections were stained using Hematoxylin-eosin (HE) staining kit (Solarbio), Masson trichrome staining kit (Solarbio), and Nissl staining kit (Methylene blue method) (Solarbio) following standard procedures. A microscope was used to record the results of the staining.

Cells were cultured in twelve-well plates for 48 h until they reached the logarithmic growth phase. ROS production was induced by treating PC12 cells with 100 μM H2O2 for 6 h or BV2 cells with 1 μg/mL LPS for 24 h. Following ROS induction, the cells were incubated with V-CDs and V-CDs@Ce at a concentration of 100 μg/mL in place of the standard medium for 12 h. Subsequently, 10 μM of the ROS fluorescent probe 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) (Solarbio) was added to each well, and the cells were incubated for an additional 30 min. The cells were then washed three times with serum-free medium to remove any excess probe. Finally, the fluorescence intensity of ROS was quantified using a fluorescence microscope.

Cell suspensions were uniformly inoculated into 96-well plates for 48 h, after which different drug concentrations were added to the wells for a 12-hour incubation. Subsequently, CCK-8 reagent was added to each well, and the plates were incubated for 3 h. An enzyme marker was used to determine the absorbance at 450 nm for each well, and cell viability values were calculated as per the instructions of the Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) Cell Proliferation and Cytotoxicity Assay Kit (Solarbio).

Cell suspensions were inoculated into twelve-well plates 48 h and treatment with H2O2 and the drug. Red and green fluorescence intensities were recorded by fluorescence microscopy after an MMP probe (Beyotime) was added to each well.

Cells were treated with H2O2 and drugs, and cell and supernatant samples were collected and centrifuged to remove the culture medium. Appropriate reagents were added following the steps of the Annexin V-FITC Apoptosis Detection Kit (Beyotime), and flow cytometer (Agilent) was used to assess the rate of apoptosis.

Spinal cord tissue from traumatic SCI mice treated for 3 days was removed, and cells were treated with H2O2 and drugs. Samples were fixed with glutaraldehyde, stained with osmium acid, and prepared for transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Lead citrate staining solution and dioxin acetate staining solution were used to stain the sections, which were then observed by TEM (JEM-1200EX).
The spinal cord was removed 3 days after treatment and the treated PC12 cells were collected. ATP levels were determined according to the instructions of the ATP Assay Kit (Beyotime).

Cell culture supernatants and tissue homogenates were collected and analyzed for various cytokines and proteins using ELISA kits specific for interleukin 6 (IL-6), interleukin 1β (IL-1β), tumor necrosis factor α (TNF-α), interleukin-10 (IL-10). The kits used included Mouse IL-6 ELISA KIT (Solarbio), Mouse IL-1β ELISA KIT (Solarbio), Mouse TNF-α ELISA KIT (Solarbio), Mouse IL-10 ELISA KIT (Solarbio).

The anti-oxidative stress capacity was evaluated by detecting Glutathione (GSH), Glutathione Peroxidase (GSH-Px/GPX), Malondialdehyde (MDA), Total Superoxide Dismutase (T-SOD), Peroxidase (POD), Catalase (CAT), and Total Antioxidant Capacity (T-AOC) levels in PC12 cells and spinal cord tissues. Kits used included Reduced GSH Colorimetric Assay Kit (Elabscience), Glutathione Peroxidase (GPX) Activity Assay Kit (Solarbio), T-SOD Activity Assay Kit (WST-1 Method) (Elabscience), MDA Fluorometric Assay Kit (Elabscience), POD Activity Assay Kit (Solarbio), CAT Activity Assay Kit (Elabscience), and T-AOC Colorimetric Assay Kit (ABTS, Chemical Method) (Elabscience).

Western blot analysis was performed using the antibodies anti-Adenosine 5‘-monophosphate-activated protein kinase (AMPK) (affinity), anti-P-AMPK (affinity), anti-Peroxisome proliIerators-activated receptor γ coactivator l alpha (PGC-1α) (affinity), anti-Nrf-2 (affinity), anti-β-actin (Proteintech), HRP Affinity Goat Anti-Mouse IgG, and HRP Affinity Goat Anti-Rabbit IgG (Proteintech). The Tanon 2500R gel imaging system (Tanon, China) was used to record the relative protein blots, and ImageJ 1.39 V software was employed to quantify the relative protein blots.

After 28 days of drug injection, blood biochemical indicators, including blood urea nitrogen (BUN), creatinine (Cr), alanine transaminase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), total cholesterol (T-CHOL), total Bilirubin (TBIL), creatine Kinase (CK), glucose (GLU), triglyceride (TG), total protein (TP), and albumin (ALB), were evaluated using mouse serum in an SMT-120VP animal biochemistry analyzer (Seamaty). Blood routine indices including white blood cell (WBC), red blood cell (RBC), hemoglobin (HGB), hematocrit (HCT), mean corpuscular volume (MCV), mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH), mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC), mean platelet volume (MPV), and platelet (PLT) were assessed in the ProCyte Dx Hematology Analyzer (IDEXX) using mouse whole blood.

Motor function assessment was conducted using the BMS scoring system. The mice were scored by three individuals in a double-blind manner. After 28 days of treatment, each group of mice had their front paws marked with black pigment and their hind paws with red pigment. They were then observed while walking in the indicated direction, and mouse tracks were recorded. After 28 days, the wet weight of the gastrecnemius muscle of mice was measured and made into paraffin sections for HE staining to observe the muscle atrophy and record the cross-sectional area (CSA) of the myofibre.

The fresh spinal cord tissue was taken out and placed into a test tube, 1 mL of nitric acid was added into the tube, and dissolved by heating in an oven at 120 degrees for 30 min. The metal ion content was measured using an ICP-MS (Perkin Elmer NexION 300D ICP-MS, USA).

All experimental data in this study are presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Statistical analysis was carried out using one-way ANOVA and Tukey's multiple comparison tests in GraphPad Prism software to compare differences among groups. Statistical significance was considered at P < 0.05.

From the low and high magnification TEM images (Fig. 1 a-d), we observe a distinct size difference between V-CDs and V-CDs@Ce. The V-CDs have an average diameter of approximately 2 nm, whereas V-CDs@Ce are larger, around 4 nm. This size increase suggests that the introduction of Ce during synthesis has induced changes in the structural configuration of the carbon dots. Despite the variation in size, the Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis) absorption spectra (Fig. 1e) and Fourier Transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Fig. 1f) of both V-CDs and V-CDs@Ce show similar peak positions for major functional groups, indicating that their core chemical structures remain largely consistent, with no substantial modification to the primary bonding environment or surface functionalization. Key functional groups, such as N–H and C-O, are clearly identifiable in the IR spectra, suggesting that these functional groups are preserved after Ce incorporation.

The 1H NMR analysis (Fig. 1g) provides further insight into the structural properties of V-CDs and V-CDs@Ce. While both show similar functional groups like hydroxyl (–OH), ether (−OR), nitro (–NO2), and NH-containing groups, subtle differences in chemical shifts reveal variations in their electronic environments. The electron-withdrawing nature of these substituents, particularly those like –NO2, shifts the hydrogen peaks on the Cα (the carbon directly bonded to the substituent) to a lower field by approximately 2–4 ppm. Hydrogens on Cβ (the carbon adjacent to Cα) are also deshielded, resulting in downfield shifts of about 1–2 ppm. In the case of sp2 hybridized carbons, such as those in carbonyl (C = O), alkenes (C = C), and aromatic rings, Cα hydrogens experience a downfield shift of 1–2 ppm due to the electron-deficient environment. In the 4–6 ppm range of the 1H NMR spectrum, chemical shifts are typically associated with protons attached to electronegative atoms or sp2 hybridized carbons. Specifically, the chemical shifts around 4 ppm are often indicative of protons in environments such as methoxy (–OCH3) or other alkoxy (−OR) groups, where the oxygen atom causes deshielding. Similarly, shifts near 6 ppm suggest the presence of protons on sp2 hybridized carbons, such as those in alkenes (C = C) or aromatic rings, where the electron-withdrawing nature of the adjacent π-system results in downfield shifts. These observations point to the likelihood of alkoxy groups and unsaturated systems, like aromatic or vinyl groups, contributing to the signals in this region of the spectrum.These shifts observed in the NMR spectra confirm that the introduction of Ce may lead to changes in the electronic distribution within the carbon structure, even though the primary functional groups remain similar.

The fluorescence lifetime measurements (Fig. 1h) further support the similarity in photophysical properties between V-CDs and V-CDs@Ce, with both exhibiting lifetimes in the nanosecond range. However, the enhanced fluorescence intensity of V-CDs@Ce compared to V-CDs (Fig. 1i) suggests that the spatial arrangement and electronic structure of the carbon dots have been significantly influenced by the Ce incorporation during synthesis. This enhancement in fluorescence, despite the absence of Ce in the final material, points to a memory effect, where Ce temporarily interacts with the structure, inducing changes that persist even after its removal. This memory effect likely involves a reorganization of the polyphenolic matrix, enhancing its electronic properties and leading to greater fluorescence.

XPS analysis (Fig. 1j) sheds more light on the electronic structure. Both V-CDs and V-CDs@Ce exhibit similar oxidation states for carbon, with high-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. k-l) confirming the presence of sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbons. However, the absence of detectable Ce in V-CDs@Ce, even after increasing Ce loading (Fig. S1), suggests that Ce is not physically retained in the final product. The results from XPS conclusively demonstrate that Ce is removed during synthesis, reinforcing the idea that the observed structural and photophysical changes are due to a catalytic memory effect rather than elemental doping. This memory-driven Ce enhancement modifies the electronic environment of the carbon dots, leading to improved properties without the potential drawbacks associated with residual Ce, such as reduced biocompatibility or toxicity.

Further confirmation of the Ce memory effect is provided by the superior antioxidant enzyme-like activity of V-CDs@Ce, as shown in Fig. 1 (m-o). The enhanced ability to scavenge ROS, including singlet oxygen (1O2), hydroxyl radicals (•OH), and superoxide anions (O2•-), highlights the catalytic efficiency of V-CDs@Ce compared to V-CDs. This improved ROS-scavenging activity can be attributed to the structural changes induced by Ce during synthesis, which enhance the interaction of the carbon dots with ROS. These changes mimic the catalytic properties of Ce, allowing V-CDs@Ce to neutralize oxidative species more effectively without the direct presence of Ce.

The implications of these findings are profound, particularly for biomedical applications such as the treatment of traumatic SCI. Oxidative stress is a major contributor to neuronal degeneration in traumatic SCI, and the enhanced ROS-scavenging capabilities of V-CDs@Ce could significantly reduce oxidative damage, facilitating recovery and tissue regeneration. Additionally, the absence of retained Ce ensures the biocompatibility of V-CDs@Ce, making them ideal for in vivo applications where safety is paramount. By leveraging the catalytic memory effect of Ce, V-CDs@Ce offer a novel and promising strategy for combating oxidative stress, not only in SCI but also in a range of ROS-related conditions.

To further explore the potential of V-CDs@Ce in treating traumatic SCI, we aim to delve deeper into the underlying mechanisms driving its therapeutic advantages. For instance, the observed enhancement in ROS-scavenging activity, combined with the material’s ability to trigger specific key biochemical pathways. These mechanisms likely may contribute to the material’s ability to accelerate tissue repair, promote neuronal regeneration, and restore cellular homeostasis. We plan to undertake a series of studies to investigate how the Ce memory effect-driven design of V-CDs@Ce can further enhance recovery in traumatic SCI. These studies will explore its advantages in mitigating inflammation, promoting vascular and skin regeneration, and facilitating long-term functional recovery, positioning V-CDs@Ce as a promising tool for addressing the complexities of traumatic SCI and related conditions.

Traumatic SCI often result in apoptotic necrosis of neuronal cells, a process primarily driven by the escalation of harmful factors, particularly the oxidative stress present in the local microenvironment [42]. Research suggests that neonatal neurons possess a certain degree of regenerative capacity, while the regenerative potential of adult neurons remains severely constrained [43]. This limitation significantly complicates the treatment of traumatic SCI, highlighting the critical importance of mitigating neuronal apoptosis following such injuries.

Extensive studies have established that the elevated oxidative environment at the injury site is a key driver of neuronal apoptosis [44]. To model this condition, we utilized H2O2 to induce apoptosis in vitro. We selected the PC12 cell line for our experiments and employed flow cytometry to quantify the rate of apoptosis. Our results revealed that after inducing apoptosis with H2O2, the average apoptosis rate in PC12 cells reached 19.12 %. However, this rate significantly decreased to 12.16 % following treatment with V-CDs and further reduced to just 5.98 % after treatment with V-CDs@Ce (as shown in Fig. 2 a-b).

Caspases, particularly caspase-3, are critical mediators of apoptosis [45]. The activation of caspase-3 and the presence of its cleaved form (cleaved caspase-3) are widely recognized markers of apoptotic processes [46]. In our study, we employed IF to assess the expression of cleaved caspase-3 in PC12 cells. Following H2O2 induction, the fluorescence intensity of cleaved caspase-3 significantly increased, and neuronal axons were nearly obliterated. In contrast, the V-CDs treatment group showed a reduction in fluorescence intensity and partial restoration of neuronal axons. Notably, the V-CDs@Ce treatment group exhibited the most pronounced decrease in fluorescence intensity, accompanied by substantial recovery of neuronal axons (as depicted in Fig. 2c, e).

To further elucidate the role of V-CDs@Ce in mitigating neuronal apoptosis, we conducted double IF labeling of the injured spinal cord tissues in each group, utilizing Neun as a neuronal marker and cleaved caspase-3 as an apoptosis marker. The results were consistent with our in vitro findings, showing a significant decrease in both cleaved caspase-3 fluorescence intensity and the number of Neun+/cleaved caspase-3+ cells. These observations demonstrate the potent anti-apoptotic effects of V-CDs@Ce in vivo, thereby reinforcing its therapeutic potential (as shown in Fig. 2d, f, and S2). The above results indicate that V-CDs@Ce significantly inhibit neuronal apoptosis, suggesting a promising therapeutic approach for the treatment of traumatic SCI. These findings underscore the potential of V-CDs@Ce to address the complex challenge of traumatic SCI by targeting the oxidative stress and apoptotic pathways that are central to neuronal degeneration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Alleviating neuronal mitochondrial damage and mitochondrial oxidative stress
ce:section.ce:para: While a certain level of ROS is essential for initiating and sustaining regenerative processes within the body, excessive ROS production in spinal cord tissues following traumatic SCI can lead to apoptosis and, in severe cases, necrosis due to cellular oxidative stress [47]. Monitoring ROS levels is crucial not only for assessing normal cellular function but also for gauging the extent of cellular damage induced by environmental factors. Therefore, reducing intracellular ROS levels is a primary goal in mitigating neuronal damage following traumatic SCI [48].

To evaluate ROS levels in our study, we used the DCFH-DA fluorescent probe, which binds to ROS and emits green fluorescence. Our results showed that PC12 cells exhibited intense green fluorescence after being injured with H2O2, indicating high ROS levels. However, this fluorescence was significantly reduced following treatment with V-CDs, and even further decreased after treatment with V-CDs@Ce, demonstrating the effectiveness of these treatments in reducing ROS levels (as depicted in Fig. 3 a-b).

The primary sources of ROS in animal cells include the mitochondrial respiratory chain, macrophages, and lipid peroxidation of mitochondrial polyunsaturated membranes [49]. Mitochondria, often referred to as the “powerhouse of the cell,” are central to eukaryotic cell survival and death processes due to their role in energy production and participation in various cellular activities, including inter-cellular signaling, differentiation, and the life-death cycle [50]. Following traumatic SCI, depolarization of the inner mitochondrial membrane can lead to the opening of the mitochondrial permeability transition pore (PTP), which causes the release of pro-apoptotic proteins, including cytochrome C, thereby triggering apoptosis[51]. ROS exacerbate this process by further disrupting the MMP, accelerating cell death[52]. MMP disruption is considered one of the earliest events in the apoptotic cascade, occurring even before nuclear apoptotic features such as chromatin condensation and deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) fragmentation appear. Once MMP collapses, apoptosis becomes irreversible, emphasizing the importance of maintaining MMP stability and protecting neuronal mitochondria during traumatic SCI repair[53].

To assess MMP, we used the JC-1 staining kit, which produces red fluorescence under normal conditions and green fluorescence when MMP decreases. Our findings revealed that after H2O2-induced injury, PC12 cells showed higher green fluorescence, lower red fluorescence, and significantly decreased MMP levels. However, MMP levels were restored following treatment with V-CDs, with the most substantial recovery observed in the V-CDs@Ce treatment group (as shown in Fig. 3c-d).

To further investigate mitochondrial recovery, we utilized TEM to examine the mitochondrial microstructure in PC12 cells (Fig. 3e) and spinal cord tissues (Figure S3a) across all experimental groups. The results demonstrated that V-CDs@Ce most effectively restored mitochondrial matrix integrity, reduced swelling, and alleviated cristae shortening disorder.

Following traumatic SCI, disruptions in the electrochemical gradient generated by the proton pump in the mitochondrial membrane can impair ATP production, leading to energy depletion and subsequent cellular damage[54]. To address this, we measured ATP levels in PC12 cells (Fig. 3f) and damaged spinal cord tissues (Figure S3b) using an ATP assay kit. The results clearly demonstrated that V-CDs@Ce significantly restored ATP levels in both cells and tissues.

ROS in the body include various species such as the O2•-, H2O2, •OH, and 1O2, all of which are highly reactive due to their unpaired electrons[55]. To counteract the harmful effects of ROS, the body employs a range of antioxidant enzymes[56]. SOD rapidly converts O2•- into H2O2, which is then decomposed into non-toxic H2O by CAT [57]. GSH acts as a hydrogen donor, while GSH-Px/GPX specifically catalyzes the reaction between GSH and H2O2, producing oxidized glutathione disulfide (GSSG) and water, thus reducing peroxide formation and maintaining cellular homeostasis[58].

We began our analysis by evaluating the T-AOC in both cells (Fig. 3g) and spinal cord tissues (Figure S4). The data showed an increase in T-AOC levels following treatment with V-CDs, with an even more pronounced increase in the V-CDs@Ce group. Subsequent investigations assessed the levels of key antioxidant enzymes, including GSH, GSH-Px/GPX, SOD, POD, and CAT in cells (Fig. 3g). Notably, all of these enzymes exhibited significantly higher activity in the V-CDs@Ce group compared to the V-CDs group. Consistent results were observed in spinal cord tissues (Figure S4), confirming the enhanced antioxidant enzyme activity induced by V-CDs@Ce. Following traumatic SCI, neuronal cells are prone to membrane lipid peroxidation, with MDA being the final breakdown product of this process. MDA accumulation can cause membrane damage, making it a critical parameter for assessing oxidative stress[59]. Our findings indicated lower MDA levels in PC12 cells (Fig. 3g) and spinal cord tissues (Figure S4) in both the V-CDs and V-CDs@Ce groups, with the V-CDs@Ce group showing the lowest MDA levels.

In summary, V-CDs@Ce significantly mitigated neuronal mitochondrial damage and oxidative stress, suggesting a promising therapeutic strategy for traumatic SCI treatment. These findings highlight the ability of V-CDs@Ce to not only reduce ROS levels but also to restore mitochondrial function and enhance the body's antioxidant defenses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Promotes microglia/macrophage polarization from the M1 to M2 phenotype and exerts anti-inflammatory effects
ce:section.ce:para: Under normal physiological conditions, microglia/macrophage remain in a quiescent state[60]. However, following traumatic SCI, these cells undergo a dynamic transformation into two distinct phenotypes: the pro-inflammatory M1 phenotype and the anti-inflammatory M2 phenotype[61]. The balance between these phenotypes is crucial and varies with the progression of the injury[62]. Pro-inflammatory M1 cells contribute to secondary inflammatory responses by releasing cytokines such as IL-6, IL-1β, and TNF-α, which exacerbate tissue damage. Conversely, M2 cells play a protective role by clearing cellular debris and secreting anti-inflammatory mediators and neurotrophic factors like IL-10, thereby promoting neuronal protection and tissue repair[63].

While the role of inflammation in traumatic SCI is well-established, achieving a balanced regulation between M1 and M2 phenotypes remains a significant challenge[64]. The regulation of intracellular ROS is believed to be a critical factor in maintaining this balance, as elevated ROS levels are often associated with the activation of M1 cells, while reducing ROS can promote M2 polarization.

To explore this, we stimulated BV2 microglial cells with LPS to induce ROS production and measured intracellular ROS levels using a ROS-specific fluorescent probe. Our results showed that V-CDs@Ce significantly reduced intracellular ROS levels compared to V-CDs, indicating a stronger antioxidative effect (Fig. 4 a-b).

To further investigate the impact on microglial polarization, we performed an IF assay to assess the expression of INOS, a marker for M1 cells, and Arg-1, a marker for M2 cells. The findings revealed that treatment with V-CDs@Ce significantly decreased INOS expression while increasing Arg-1 expression compared to both the LPS and V-CDs groups (Fig. 4c-f). These results suggest that V-CDs@Ce effectively promotes the shift from the M1 to the M2 phenotype in vitro.

To corroborate these findings, we quantified the levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-1β, and TNF-α) secreted by M1-type cells, as well as the anti-inflammatory cytokine IL-10 secreted by M2-type cells, using ELISA. Our data indicated that the LPS group exhibited the highest levels of IL-6, IL-1β, and TNF-α, with a subsequent decrease in the V-CDs group, and the lowest levels in the V-CDs@Ce group. Conversely, IL-10 levels were highest in the V-CDs@Ce group (Fig. 4g-j). These trends were consistent in the spinal cord tissues analyzed (Figure S5).

Additionally, we performed IHC to examine the expression of INOS and Arg-1 in spinal cord tissues. After traumatic SCI, INOS expression was significantly elevated, with lower levels observed in the V-CDs group and the lowest expression in the V-CDs@Ce group (Fig. 4k). In contrast, Arg-1 expression followed an inverse pattern, with the highest levels observed in the V-CDs@Ce group (Figure S6).

These findings collectively demonstrate that V-CDs@Ce possesses a superior capacity to promote the polarization of microglial/macrophage from the M1 to the M2 phenotype. This shift effectively balances the inflammatory response, suggesting that V-CDs@Ce could be a potent therapeutic agent for mitigating inflammation and promoting tissue repair following traumatic SCI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Promote motor function recovery and improve pathological changes in traumatic SCI mice
ce:section.ce:para: The traumatic SCI mouse model was established using the PSI HI-0400 spinal cord impactor (Figure S7 and Fig. 5 a), followed by a 28-day treatment regimen with V-CDs and V-CDs@Ce. Evaluating the therapeutic effects of these treatments focused primarily on motor function recovery and pathological changes, providing direct and comprehensive insights into their efficacy.

To assess motor function recovery, we meticulously recorded the footprints of mice from each treatment group. The traumatic SCI-afflicted mice displayed erratic footprints with significant hind limb dragging. Mice in the V-CDs group exhibited some readiness for walking, although they faced considerable difficulty. In contrast, mice in the V-CDs@Ce group demonstrated significantly smoother and more coordinated walking patterns (Fig. 5b). To further quantify motor function recovery, we measured the step length and step width of each group. The results showed that mice treated with V-CDs@Ce had a longer step length and narrower step width compared to those treated with V-CDs alone (Figure S8).

The BMS score, a widely recognized metric for evaluating motor function recovery in rodent models of traumatic SCI, was employed to further assess the efficacy of the treatments. Over a four-week period, the traumatic SCI group’s BMS score improved from 0 to 2, the V-CDs group’s score increased from 0 to 4, and the V-CDs@Ce group achieved a significant improvement from 0 to 6.3 (Fig. 5c). The combination of BMS scores and footprint analysis strongly underscored the superior impact of V-CDs@Ce in facilitating motor function recovery.

Histopathological analyses were conducted to corroborate these functional assessments. Paraffin sections approximately 0.2 cm from the injury site were subjected to HE and Nissl staining. HE staining revealed extensive tissue degeneration and necrosis in the traumatic SCI group, with these pathological features being notably reduced in the V-CDs group. Remarkably, tissue in the V-CDs@Ce group exhibited characteristics approaching those of normal spinal cord tissue (Fig. 5d).

Nissl staining further corroborated these findings by demonstrating a near-complete loss of Nissl bodies in the SCI group, only limited remnants in the V-CDs treated group, and a marked preservation of Nissl bodies in the V-CDs@Ce group (Fig. 5e and S9a). This suggests that V-CDs@Ce significantly protects neuronal integrity and prevents neuronal loss induced by SCI.

To provide a more comprehensive evaluation of the therapeutic effects, HE staining was conducted on longitudinal sections of the spinal cord to assess the extent and severity of tissue injury. Quantitative and qualitative analyses of the HE-stained sections revealed that the V-CDs@Ce treated group exhibited minimal tissue damage, characterized by reduced lesion size and preserved spinal cord architecture, in comparison to the SCI and V-CDs groups (Figure S9b-c). These results further validate the superior therapeutic efficacy of V-CDs@Ce in mitigating SCI and promoting tissue preservation.

Given that impaired motor function following traumatic SCI often leads to hind limb muscle atrophy, we also assessed the extent of muscle atrophy by examining the gastrocnemius muscle. We removed, weighed, and conducted HE staining on the gastrocnemius muscle of the hind limbs. The results showed severe atrophy, minimal CSA, and significant weight loss in the gastrocnemius muscle of mice in the traumatic SCI group. In the V-CDs group, there was some improvement in muscle mass and structure. However, in the V-CDs@Ce group, the gastrocnemius muscle showed near-complete recovery, with significantly increased muscle weight (Fig. 5f-h).

As a result, V-CDs@Ce significantly enhanced motor function recovery and mitigated histopathological damage in traumatic SCI mice, demonstrating its potential as a highly effective treatment for traumatic SCI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Regulate the homeostasis of various metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Tissue ion homeostasis is fundamental for maintaining normal cellular metabolism and physiological functions[65]. Building upon our observation that V-CDs@Ce can mitigate inflammation, reduce oxidative stress, and prevent apoptosis and mitochondrial damage, we further investigated alterations in major metal ion concentrations in tissues post-traumatic SCI. Through the comprehensive analysis of key metal cations in the spinal cord—including calcium, copper, iron, sodium, manganese, magnesium, zinc, selenium, lithium, and potassium—we found that V-CDs significantly decreased the levels of calcium, copper, iron and sodium, while simultaneously increasing the levels of manganese, magnesium, zinc, selenium, lithium and potassium. Notably, V-CDs@Ce exhibited an even more pronounced effect (Fig. 6 a-j).

To elucidate the mechanisms by which V-CDs@Ce restores metal ion homeostasis, we focused on the Nrf-2 pathway as a potential mediator. Nrf-2 is a pivotal transcription factor involved in cellular defense against oxidative stress and exogenous toxins, playing a vital role in maintaining cellular homeostasis[66,67]. Upon activation, Nrf-2 is upregulated and translocates to the nucleus, where it induces the transcription of genes involved in antioxidant defenses and metal ion regulation[68,69]. For instance, Nrf-2 modulates iron homeostasis by decreasing ferritin synthesis and enhancing ferritin degradation, thereby reducing the concentration of ferrous ions within cells[70,71]. Additionally, Nrf-2 regulates intracellular copper balance by controlling the expression of metallothionein, the activity of copper transporters, and the oxidation state of copper[72]. Furthermore, Nrf-2 indirectly influences zinc distribution and metabolism by regulating the expression of antioxidant enzymes and metallothionein[73].

However, the specific mechanism by which Nrf-2 directly regulates intracellular sodium remains unclear, indicating a need for further investigation into the direct correlations between Nrf-2 activity and sodium ion balance within cells. Future studies should aim to elucidate this relationship to fully understand the comprehensive role of Nrf-2 in metal ion homeostasis.

Given the central role of Nrf-2 in metal ion regulation, we hypothesized that its activation is a key factor in V-CDs@Ce’s ability to restore metal ion balance in traumatic SCI. IF analysis of Nrf-2 expression in PC12 cells revealed a significant upregulation of Nrf-2 levels. Notably, while treatment with V-CDs primarily induced Nrf-2 nuclear translocation with minimal cytoplasmic changes, the addition of V-CDs@Ce further enhanced Nrf-2 localization in both the cytoplasm and the nucleus (Fig. 6k-l). This suggests that V-CDs@Ce not only activates Nrf-2 more robustly but may also facilitate its broader functional distribution within the cell, thereby amplifying its protective effects.

Emerging evidence indicates that the AMPK/PGC-1α signaling pathway plays a critical regulatory role in various aspects of mitochondrial biology and cellular homeostasis [74–78]. Considering that the AMPK/PGC-1α pathway significantly influences Nrf-2 regulation[79], we posited that AMPK might play a decisive role in modulating Nrf-2 activity in the context of traumatic SCI. To validate this hypothesis, we assessed the relative protein levels in spinal cord tissues using Western blotting. Intriguingly, the upregulation of Nrf-2 was attenuated following the introduction of Dorsomorphin, an AMPK inhibitor, suggesting that the upregulation of Nrf-2 is mediated by the AMPK/PGC-1α signaling pathway (Fig. 6m-n). The Western blot results were consistent with the IF findings, thereby reinforcing our hypothesis that Nrf-2 promotes recovery from traumatic SCI in mice by rebalancing metal ion homeostasis through the AMPK/PGC-1α signaling axis.

Moreover, considering the pivotal role of mitochondria in metal ion homeostasis, we hypothesize that V-CDs@Ce may exert its effects by enhancing mitochondrial function. Mitochondria are not only the primary energy producers in cells but also critical regulators of metal ion metabolism. V-CDs@Ce could potentially protect mitochondrial membrane integrity, reduce mitochondrial ROS production, and regulate metal ion transport channels on the mitochondrial membrane. These actions would collectively contribute to the restoration of metal ion balance and overall cellular homeostasis.

Overall, our findings provide compelling evidence that targeting the AMPK/PGC-1α/Nrf-2 axis is a promising therapeutic strategy for improving outcomes in traumatic SCI. By comprehensively managing metal ion imbalances and supporting mitochondrial function, V-CDs@Ce demonstrates significant therapeutic potential. These insights not only advance our understanding of the molecular mechanisms underlying SCI recovery but also pave the way for the development of novel therapeutic interventions aimed at restoring cellular homeostasis and promoting tissue regeneration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Facilitation of wound healing in traumatic spinal cord injury mouse models
ce:section.ce:para: Trauma is the leading cause of SCI, often accompanied by wounds. While a controlled inflammatory response is crucial for clearing necrotic tissue and facilitating wound healing, the presence of a wound can lead to excessive inflammation[80]. This heightened inflammatory response can slow or even impede wound healing, particularly when traumatic SCI is also present[81].

Macrophages play a central role in skin wound repair, shifting between M1-type (pro-inflammatory) and M2-type (anti-inflammatory) phenotypes during different phases of the healing process[82–84]. This transition is essential for restoring fibroblast function and promoting tissue repair. Increasing the proportion of M2-type macrophages in skin wounds enhances the secretion of anti-inflammatory cytokines, such as IL-10, and pro-angiogenic factors like CD31, which collectively promote angiogenesis and accelerate wound healing[85].

Given the established role of macrophages in wound healing, our investigation extended to spinal cord tissues, where we observed that treatment with V-CDs and V-CDs@Ce reduced the secretion of pro-inflammatory factors by inflammatory cells while promoting M2-type macrophage polarization. This led us to hypothesize that these treatments could also enhance wound healing in SCI mice with concurrent skin injuries.

To test this hypothesis, we created 1.5 × 1.5 cm wounds on the backs of SCI mice and closely monitored the healing process. Our observations revealed that skin wounds without concomitant SCI healed significantly faster than those with SCI, suggesting that SCI delays wound healing. Remarkably, skin wounds with SCI treated with V-CDs@Ce healed more rapidly than untreated skin wounds without SCI, underscoring the therapeutic potential of V-CDs@Ce (see Fig. 7 b-c).

Histological analysis through HE staining revealed that SCI-related skin wounds exhibited an incomplete epithelial structure and poor scar tissue repair, characterized by a lower count of epithelial fibroblasts. In contrast, skin wounds without SCI showed a continuous epithelial structure, with increased numbers of epithelial fibroblasts and blood vessels. Treatment with V-CDs@Ce further accelerated healing in SCI-related skin wounds, as evidenced by enhanced fibrous connective tissue proliferation observed through Masson staining (see Fig. 7d).

IHC analysis further demonstrated increased angiogenesis in wounds treated with V-CDs@Ce, highlighting the compound's pro-healing effects (see Fig. 7e). In light of these findings, we measured the levels of cytokines IL-6, IL-1β, TNF-α, and IL-10, confirming V-CDs@Ce's superior anti-inflammatory properties (see Fig. 7f-i). IHC analysis of skin tissues demonstrated that treatment with V-CDs@Ce significantly reduced iNOS expression while markedly increasing Arg-1 expression (see Figure S10). These findings indicate that V-CDs@Ce effectively promotes the polarization of skin macrophages toward the anti-inflammatory M2 phenotype.

Overall, V-CDs@Ce significantly enhances wound healing in traumatic SCI, suggesting its potential as a therapeutic agent for treating combined injuries involving spinal cord trauma and skin wounds. This study underscores the importance of macrophage modulation in improving healing outcomes in complex trauma cases.

In the treatment of both traumatic SCI and the corresponding wounds, the safety and potential contraindications of drug compounds are critical factors that can significantly influence treatment outcomes. Ensuring that synthesized drugs exhibit excellent biosafety is fundamental to their therapeutic efficacy. Therefore, rigorous evaluation of the biosafety of the synthesized compounds, V-CDs and V-CDs@Ce, is essential to establish their viability as safe treatment options.

To evaluate the biosafety of V-CDs and V-CDs@Ce, we conducted a series of in vitro and in vivo experiments. First, we assessed the cytotoxicity of these compounds on PC12 and BV2 cells using the CCK8 assay, exposing the cells to concentrations ranging from 0 to 800 μg/mL. The results demonstrated that even at the highest concentration of 800 μg/mL, cell viability remained above 80 %, indicating low cytotoxicity and suggesting that V-CDs and V-CDs@Ce are well-tolerated by these cell lines (Figure S11a).

Next, we examined the hemolytic potential of V-CDs and V-CDs@Ce by exposing red blood cells to a concentration of 400 μg/mL. The hemolysis assay revealed no significant hemolytic activity, further supporting the blood compatibility of these compounds (Figure S11b).

In vivo, we investigated the systemic safety of V-CDs@Ce by administering repeated tail vein injections in mice. Comprehensive blood routine analyses (Figure S12) and blood biochemical tests (Figure S13) showed no significant changes in key blood parameters, indicating that V-CDs@Ce does not adversely affect systemic health.

To further explore the potential impact on major organs, we performed histopathological analysis using HE staining on tissue samples from the heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys. These examinations did not reveal any significant pathological changes, suggesting that V-CDs@Ce does not induce organ toxicity (Fig. 8 ).

Given the concern that quantum dots, due to their small size, might cross the blood–brain barrier, we extended our histopathological analysis to brain tissues from the treated mice[86]. HE staining of the brain revealed no discernible long-term toxicity, indicating that V-CDs@Ce does not pose significant risks to CNS integrity (Figure S14).

In conclusion, our comprehensive biosafety evaluations indicate that V-CDs@Ce exhibits excellent long-term biocompatibility, with no significant cytotoxic, hemolytic, systemic, or neurotoxic effects. These findings underscore the potential of V-CDs@Ce as a safe and effective therapeutic agent for the simultaneous treatment of SCI and skin wounds, providing a strong foundation for further clinical development.

This study represents the first successful application of cerium’s memory effect—distinct from traditional cerium doping—to enhance the therapeutic efficacy of V-CDs@Ce for treating traumatic SCI. By leveraging cerium’s catalytic memory, V-CDs@Ce exhibit significantly enhanced enzyme-like activities that initiate essential biochemical processes for recovery. This heightened activity directly correlates with robust ROS scavenging, effectively mitigating oxidative stress—a critical factor in neuronal degeneration and impaired wound healing—and fostering cellular repair and regeneration. Our findings highlight the pivotal role of the upregulated Nrf-2 pathway, which accelerates recovery by regulating antioxidant defenses and cellular stress responses. Additionally, V-CDs@Ce facilitate mitochondrial repair through activation of the AMPK/PGC-1α/Nrf-2 signaling axis, restoring mitochondrial function and rebalancing essential metal ions to maintain cellular homeostasis during recovery. This innovative strategy, coupled with the superior biocompatibility of V-CDs@Ce, offers a novel and highly effective therapeutic solution for traumatic SCI, providing new hope for improving outcomes in individuals facing complex spinal cord injuries.
